coredata.dc:title: Chapter 34 Quantum Dots Novel Realm in Biomedical and Pharmaceutical Industry
coredata.prism:publicationName: Handbook of Nanomaterials for Industrial Applications

coredata.dc:creator: Pawar, Rohit S.
Upadhaya, Prashant G.
Patravale, Vandana B.
coredata.dc:description: Medicinal problems have always been a matter of pursuit in almost all civilizations. This has led to the requirements of developing a drug delivery system which would dispense the pharmacologically active moieties only to the site of action, without distressing the healthy tissues and organs. Since nanoscience and nanoengineering are one of the trending areas of present scientific progress, they are regarded as potential mediums for successful applications in nanomedicine and biomedical fields. Nanotechnology has affirmatively impacted the present healthcare industry. However, the early determinations in the field of nanomedicine were attentive only on taming the properties of preexisting therapeutic and diagnostic modalities. Recent years have witnessed several novel moieties with therapeutic and diagnostic potential, with the emergence of the supramolecular assembly of simple components through nanoengineering. Quantum dots (QDs) are one such example which have been successfully applied to the diagnostic and therapeutic field. QDs which were initially used in determination of surface kinetics are now successfully used as drug delivery agents as well as diagnostic markers. QDs are nanocrystals which are composed of a semiconductor core material which is further fenced inside another semiconductor material having larger spectral band gap. A typical QD generally falls in the range of 2–10nm in diameter making it functional for one on one interaction with drugs and biomolecules. Apart from their nanosize, other unique features such as broad-range excitation, high fluorescence, size-tunable narrow emission spectra, and high photostability potentiate the application potential of QDs to nanodiagnostics, imaging, targeted drug delivery, and photodynamic therapy. In this chapter, we will be focusing on various aspects of QDs: their pharmacology, toxicological manifestations, recent advances, and applications in biomedical and pharmaceutical industry.

nanoengineering
pharmaceutical application
quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of Quantum Dots in Biomedical Field
Application of Quantum Dots in Disease Diagnosis
Application of QDs in Early Diagnosis of Cancer
Techniques Employed for Detection of Cancer
Detection of Cancer Using Quantum Dots
Application of Quantum Dots in Immuno Based Studies
Application of QDs in Biolabeling and Bioimaging
Application of Quantum Dots in the Field Biosensors and Biodetection
Application of Quantum Dots in Pharmaceutical Field
QDs as Drug Delivery and Molecule Tracking Aid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of size-tunable QDs, generation of the electron–hole pair (exciton) upon photoexcitation shadowed by the radiative recombination, i.e., fluorescence emission or relaxation through the trap states [52]. QDs, quantum dots.
Near-infrared imaging system for SLNB in breast cancer surgery [60]. SLNB, sentinel lymph node biopsy.

Mechanism of action of PDT using quantum dots. (The mechanism involves instigation of a QD-PS FRET pair owing to the trigger by a particular wavelength light. Mechanism also involves creation of highly toxic singlet oxygen species which destroys the cancerous mass) [60]. PDT, photodynamic therapy; QD, quantum dot; FRET, fluorescence resonance energy transfer.

ce:figure.ce:label: Figure 34.4

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The figure depicts the comparision of the photostability between QDs and Alexa 488. (A) In the top row: The Nuclear antigens that were labeled with QD 630–streptavidin (in red color), and microtubules which were labeled with Alexa 488 conjugated to the anti-mouse IgG (in green color) concurrently in a 3T3 cell. In the bottom row: The microtubules that were categorized with QD 630–streptavidin (in red color), and nuclear antigens which were stained in green color with Alexa 488 conjugated to the antihuman IgG. The samples were unceasingly illuminated for 3min with light using a 100W mercury lamp (under a 100×1.30 oil-immersion objective lens). An excitation filter (at 485±20nm) was used as a source to excite both QD 630 and Alexa 488 and the resultant emissions were collected using emission filters at 635±10 and at 535±10nm on a mechanized filter wheel. The images were captured with the aid of a cooled CCD camera (automated 10s intermissions for each color). Images at the intervals of 0, 20, 60, 120, and 180s are shown in the figure. The labeling signals of Alexa 488 was found to fade quickly and further becoming undetectable within 2min. However, the signals from QD 630 did not show any obvious change for the entire 3min span of illumination. Scale bar, 10µm. (B) Upon quantitative analysis of the fluctuations in intensities of QD 608–streptavidin (stained microtubules) and Alexa 488–streptavidin (stained nuclear antigens) using samples mounted with glycerol/Vectashield (antifade mounting medium). All the experimental conditions were similar to that of (A) except for emission filter for QD 608 was changed to 605±10nm. The mean fluorescence intensity was measured at an interval of 10s for the span of 3min automatically. QD, quantum dot.

ce:figure.ce:source: Reprinted with permission from X. Wu, H. Liu, J. Liu, K.N. Haley, J.A. Treadway, J.P. Larson, et al., Immunofluorescent labeling of cancer marker Her2 and other cellular targets with semiconductor quantum dots, Nat. Biotechnol. 21 (2003) 41–46. Copyright 2003 Nature publishing group.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: General Methods for Fabrication of QDs

row.entry: The precursor material is generally heated or combusted at temperatures ranging from 100 to 200°C for defined time span followed by dilution with aqueous solutions. The QDs are further procured by using dialysis method
Generation of QDs is done by autoclaving the precursor material at 200–300°C for defined time span followed by dilution with aqueous solution. The QDs are further procured by using dialysis method
This method involves irradiation of the QD precursor using a microwave source having capacity to work up to 1000W power. The irradiation time however varies from precursor to precursor

This method generally involves synthesis of QDs by electro-oxidation of the precursor metal. The precursor metal is usually taken as working electrode while the counter electrode is generally platinum. The electrochemical process is carried out in an electrolytic solution by changing the potential applied

In this method, the QD precursor is first dehydrated by acid treatment and then converted into carbonaceous material by secondary acid treatment. The prepared carbonaceous material is then further passivated using appropriate compounds according to the precursor
This method involves arcing the precursor material, e.g., graphite in hydrogen, diborane, and helium atmosphere. The voltage used for arcing depends upon the precursor material used

This method generally involves ablating the annealed samples of QD precursor such as graphite using a laser source. The ablation is generally carried out at an elevated temperature and pressure in an inert atmosphere
This method involves subjecting the QD precursor to plasma treatment. The time of treatment and the radiofrequency varies from precursor to precursor
CEA, SCC, CA19-9, CYFRA21-1, NY-ESO-I, and NSE
HER2/NEU, BRCAI, CA 27.29, CA15-3, CA 125, ING-I, MUC1, NY-BR-I, ER/PR, and BRCA2
CASA, CEA, CA 15-3, CA 19-9, CA125, CA 549, HCG, MCA, MOV-I, TAG7, and p53

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: AFP, alpha-fetoprotein; SSC, squamous cell carcinoma; CEA, carcinoembryonic antigen; NSE, neuron-specific enolase; BRCA1, breast cancer 1; CA 27.29, cancer antigen 27.29; BRCA2, breast cancer 2; CA15-3, cancer antigen 15-3; CA125, cancer antigen 125; MUC1, mucins; ER/PR, estrogen receptors/progesterone receptors; CEA, carcinoembryonic antigen; HER2/NEU, human epidermal growth factor receptor 2; EGF, epidermal growth factor; CA549, cancer antigen 549; CA 19-9, cancer antigen 19-9; MCA, mucinous carcinoma associated antigen; PSA, prostate-specific antigen; HCG, human chorionic gonadotropin; CA19-9, carbohydrate antigen 19-9; CYFRA21-1, cytokeratin fragment 21-1.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Role of QDs in Cancer Detection

row.entry: When mercaptoacetic acid glazed CdSe/ZnS QDs were covalently conjugated to a protein known as transferrin, QDs were impulsively endocytosed by HeLa cancer cells retaining the fluorescence of the QDs indicating potentials of QDs as intracellular labels

Overexpression of membrane folate receptor makes FA as a potential marker for the various tumors, i.e. ovarian, prostate, breast cancers, etc. The reason being scarcity of the folate receptors on the normal tissues as compared to the cancerous cells. In one of the studies involving diagnosis of cancers overexpressing folate receptors, the character of BSA as functional tie molecule to form FA–BSA–CdTe/ZnS QD was reviewed. It was found that the FA-QD-based conjugate had higher selectivity than compared to the non FA QD formulation

Burkitt’s lymphoma is a common type of cancer prevailing in children of equatorial Africa. Ramos cell being the human Burkitt’s lymphoma cell, an assay method was established utilizing CdTe QDs for the qualitative and quantitative analysis of Ramos cells amongst the mixture of various type of cancer cells. Extraordinary fluoro intensity was observed on the surface of the Ramos cells owing to the high loading of the QD conjugate on the cell surface
Three-component DNA system comprising of QD-labeled reporter, the quencher, and MUC1 aptamer stem

MUC1 is a glycoprotein which is expressed on most of the epithelial cell surfaces and thus serves as a potential biomarker for the early diagnosis of various cancers. Cheng et al. designed a 3-component DNA system comprising of a QD-labeled reporter, a quencher, and the MUC1 aptamer stem. Here, fluorescent QDs were used to selectively detect MUC1 peptide. Fluorescence intensity was found to decrease in presence of MUC1 peptide, however strong fluorescence was observed in its absence. This allowed the detection of MUC1 in the nanomolar range

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: CdSe, cadmium selenide; ZnS, zinc sulfide; FA, folic acid; BSA, bovine serum albumin; CdTe, cadmium telluride; MUC1, mucin 1.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Role of QDs in Detection of Different Type of Cancers

row.entry: CYFRA21-1, CEA, NY-ESO-I, SCC, NSE, CA19-9

Detection of three type of lung cancer markers namely CEA, NSE, and Cyfra21-1 were achieved by coupling a quantum dot label with dual enzyme labels. This method was found to be sensitive as well as selective for the detection of multitargets as no cross-reaction was detected between the cancer markers

The concurrent detection of the two lung cancer biomarkers, i.e., NSE and CEA based upon the practice of dual-color QDs was performed using dual antigens, dual antibodies, and dual detection antibodies, which upon reacting with each other formed sandwiched complexes in homogeneous solution. The subsequent system was added with Streptavidin-treated polystyrene beads. Dual-colored QDs having emission maxima at 525 and 655nm were also added and the same reacted with the antibodies in the detection. The results of the study exhibited that the markers NSE and CEA could be sympathetically determined with detection limits up to 1.0ng/mL level using this method. This method was found to be of greater advantage as different fluorescence was delicately achieved concurrently for both NSE and CEA markers, while the consistent antibody–antigen reaction abridged the whole detection process. The process was also rendered without any cross-reaction
BRCAI, CA15-3, BRCA2, CA 125, MUC1, CA 27.29, NY-BR-I, HER2/NEU, ING-I, ER/PR
Quantum dots such as QD 630 and QD 535 have been successfully put to use for the detection of HER-2 receptors on the breast cancer cells surface (SK-BR-3)
Method for diagnosis and imaging using folate-decorated NPs of biodegradable polymers has been developed for an early stage cancer detection.

The in vitro cell uptake studies employing confocal laser scanning microscope proved that the cell uptake by MCF-7 breast cancer cells was due to the overexpression of folate receptors than NIH 3T3 fibroblast cells.

Another study involved use of three types of antibodies—anti-Her2/neu (for seizing the breast cancer cells (SK-BR3)), anti-EpCAM (employed for the labeling of the seized SK-BR3 cells), and antibody against antiEpCAM which was conjugated to QDs. These antibodies–QDs conjugates substantiated as assuring vectors for precise and subtle imaging of the cancer cells

Two antigens (tumor) were recognized on the LNCaP/PSCA prostate cancer cells utilizing immuno QDs known as iQdots. The QDs anti-HER2 iQdot 655 and anti-PSCA iQdot 800 were conjugated with tumor targeting bivalent antibody fragments cys-diabodies which are specific to HER-2 and prostate stem cell antigen. The results upon correlation demonstrated that QDs exhibit higher sensitivity for the detection of PSA in the cancerous prostate tissue as compared to the methods used in the conventional immune histochemistry techniques. Also, the long-term photostability proclaimed QDs as potential candidates for in vivo imaging

The CdSe QDs with ZnS capping in the SPCE practice demonstrated an excellent and subtle detection of PSA. The QDs conjugated with the PSA-specific antibodies were excited using a laser source to emit at a wavelength of 405nm. The detection limit of PSA by the use of this system was testified to be 10fg/mL

An immunoassay technique was developed to detect circulating colorectal cancer cells making use of magnetic beads paired with antibody of the EpCAM and monoclonal CK19 antibody proficient in separating the circulating tumor cells from that of the body fluids. The produced complexes were further tagged with streptavidin conjugated with QDs. The fluorescent signal emerging of the QDs established the detection of surface antigens on the circulating tumor cells

The human melanoma cells are overexpressed with the adhesion molecule CD146. PEG-capped CdSe/ZnS QDs capped with PEG were synthesized and conjugated with streptavidin. These were further linked to mouse antihuman CD146 and biotinylated goat antimouse IgG. The developed system was highly fluorescent and capable of labeling the overexpressed CD146 on the melanoma cells thus making it easy for their detection

Similarly, QDs were also studied for the detection of the two glycoproteins CD44 and CD133 associated with the plasma membranes of the cancer stem cells. However, a positive response for both the markers was found in case of melanoma cells only

Mn-doped CdTeSe/CdS QDs coated with lysine and further conjugated to mono- and polyclonal antibodies were studied in the field of in vivo imaging and therapeutics. The efficacious labeling of the QDs with bioconjugates, such as Panc-1 and MiaPaCa, has proved to be a helping hand in the development of nanoprobes for in vivo imaging and other therapeutic applications
Similarly, the conjugation of the tetraiodothyroacetic acid to the QDs linked with PEG was a fruitful step in the diagnostic field

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: NIH, National Institute Health; CEA, carcinoembryonic antigen; NSE, neuron-specific enolase; Cyfra21-1, cytokeratin fragment; PSA, prostate-specific antigen; EpCAM, epithelial cell adhesion molecule; CK19, cytokeratin 19.

chapter.ce:label: Chapter 34
chapter.ce:title: Quantum Dots
chapter.ce:subtitle: Novel Realm in Biomedical and Pharmaceutical Industry

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Sciences and Technology, Institute of Chemical Technology, Mumbai, Maharashtra, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Pharmaceutical Sciences and Technology, Institute of Chemical Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Medicinal problems have always been a matter of pursuit in almost all civilizations. This has led to the requirements of developing a drug delivery system which would dispense the pharmacologically active moieties only to the site of action, without distressing the healthy tissues and organs. Since nanoscience and nanoengineering are one of the trending areas of present scientific progress, they are regarded as potential mediums for successful applications in nanomedicine and biomedical fields. Nanotechnology has affirmatively impacted the present healthcare industry. However, the early determinations in the field of nanomedicine were attentive only on taming the properties of preexisting therapeutic and diagnostic modalities. Recent years have witnessed several novel moieties with therapeutic and diagnostic potential, with the emergence of the supramolecular assembly of simple components through nanoengineering. Quantum dots (QDs) are one such example which have been successfully applied to the diagnostic and therapeutic field.

QDs which were initially used in determination of surface kinetics are now successfully used as drug delivery agents as well as diagnostic markers. QDs are nanocrystals which are composed of a semiconductor core material which is further fenced inside another semiconductor material having larger spectral band gap. A typical QD generally falls in the range of 2–10nm in diameter making it functional for one on one interaction with drugs and biomolecules. Apart from their nanosize, other unique features such as broad-range excitation, high fluorescence, size-tunable narrow emission spectra, and high photostability potentiate the application potential of QDs to nanodiagnostics, imaging, targeted drug delivery, and photodynamic therapy.
In this chapter, we will be focusing on various aspects of QDs: their pharmacology, toxicological manifestations, recent advances, and applications in biomedical and pharmaceutical industry.

ce:section.ce:para: Medicinal problems have always been a matter of pursuit in almost all civilization. This has led to the requirements of developing a drug delivery system which would dispense the pharmacologically active moieties only to the site of action, without distressing the healthy tissues and organs. Since nanoscience and nanoengineering are one of the trending areas of present scientific progress, they are regarded as potential mediums for successful application in nanomedicine and biomedical fields. Nanotechnology has affirmatively impacted the present healthcare industry. However, the early determinations in the field of nanomedicine were attentive only on taming the properties of preexisting therapeutic and diagnostic modalities [1]. Recent years have witnessed several novel moieties with therapeutic and diagnostic potential, with the emergence of the supramolecular assembly of simple components through nanoengineering. Quantum dots (QDs) are one of the examples which have been successfully applied to the diagnostic and therapeutic field [2].

QDs which were initially used in determination of surface kinetics are now successfully used as drug delivery agents as well as diagnostic markers. QDs are nanocrystals (NCs), composing a semiconductor core material, which is responsible for the fundamental optical properties such as light absorption, emission, etc. The core material is further fenced inside another semiconductor material having a larger spectral band gap and the same serves to improve the optical properties of the QDs and reduce chemical attack [3,4]. These colloidal semiconductor NCs derive their special optical properties owing to a confined 3D quantum regimen. In the semiconductor material, band gap separates the valence and conduction bands. Upon photoabsorption, an excited electron travels to the conduction band from the valence band by leaving a gap in the valence band (h+). Fluorescence is emitted when the electron returns back to the valence band. However, in the short period of light absorption, the electron (e−) and valence band (h+) identify each other and are restricted to moving independently owing to the Coloumb attraction. The distance between the (e−) (h+) pair is called the excitation Bohr radius. The semiconductors when their dimensions are reduced to few nanometers, less than Bohr radius, can be said to be in a confinement quantum regimen and thus called QDs [5].

QDs are generally constructed from elements of Group II (e.g., Zn, Cd), -VI (e.g., Se, S), III–V, and IV–VI of the periodic table. Various approaches for fabrication of QDs exist, which can broadly be classified into top-down and bottom-up. The top-down approach generally refers to the breakdown of larger carbonaceous moieties, such as carbon nanotubes [6–8], graphite [7,9–11], etc. The bottom-up method resembles carbonization along with a means employed to block nanoparticle aggregation at high temperatures. A list of methods (Table 34.1 ) employed for QD generation includes heating/combustion method [12–14], hydrothermal technique [15,16], microwave/ultrasonic assisted method [17–19], electrochemical synthesis [9,11,20], acid oxidation [21–23], arc discharge [6], laser ablation [24,25], and plasma treatment [26,27].

Postfabrication of QDs the next step involves hydrophilization of QDs as the fabricated QDs are generally very hydrophobic and are soluble in nonpolar solvents only. Hydrophilization renders QDs biocompatible for efficient application in bio and analytical applications. Hydrophilization involves three basic strategies, the first being ligand exchange method in which the original hydrophobic coating is removed and replaced with a hydrophilic bifunctional molecule, e.g., mercaptocarbonic acids. One end of the bifunctional molecule is linked to the QD surface while the other hydrophilic end is left for bioconjugation. The second strategy involves silanization and the third is based on encapsulation of the hydrophobic QDs in different carrier vehicles, e.g., amphiphilic polymers [28]. The QDs can be further characterized for their size and structure, which is generally done using scanning transmission electron microscopy, scanning electron microscopy, transmission electron microscopy, dynamic light scattering technique, etc. The optical characterization is done using ultraviolet (UV)–visible spectroscopy or photoluminescence spectroscopy. Other characterizations such as scattering and conductivity are also done, depending upon the motive of fabrication, by using appropriate techniques [4].

A typical QD falls in the range of 2–10nm in diameter making it functional for one on one interaction with drugs and biomolecules. Apart from nanosize, other unique features, such as high photostability, broad range excitation, and size tunable narrow emission spectra potentiates the application potential of QDs to nanodiagnostics, imaging, targeted drug delivery, and photodynamic therapy (PDT). In this chapter, we shall discuss the potentials of QDs in the pharmaceutical and biomedical fields.

Physicochemical properties of QDs are as follows:
1.
QDs are more photostable as compared to traditional dyes. This is due to their inorganic composition and the fluorescence intensity they work on [29].
2.
QDs are 10–20 times brighter as compared to the conventional organic dyes [30].
3.
They are less prone to degradation as compared to other optical imaging probes. This helps in tracking cellular processes effectively and for long periods of time [31].
4.
They have higher signal to noise ratio than organic dyes [29].
5.
QDs have broader excitation spectra and a narrow, sharply defined emission peak [32].
6.
They have comparably longer fluorescence, high photoresistance [30], large stokes shift, and sharp emission spectra [29,30].
7.
Molding of QDs in any shape is easy and they can be coated with a range of biomaterials [33].
8.
QDs give better contrast with electron microscope [34].
9.
QDs have novel optical and electronic properties because of quantum confinement of electrons and photons in the nanostructure [35].
10.
Confinement of quantum results in a widening of band gap which eventually increases when size of nanostructure is decreased further [36].
11.
QDs possess properties of luminescence:

QDs due to their exceptional properties like high photostability, size tunable narrow emission spectra, broad range excitation provide considerable value in various applications [33]. Their size and composition can be manipulated to get the desired emission properties, making them amenable to simultaneous detection of multiple targets. Absorption of photons by QDs takes place when excitation energy exceeds band gap. Postabsorption of this energy, electrons shift from the ground state to the excited state [37]. The energy associated with optical absorption and electronic structure of material is directly related with each other.

The fate of QDs such as in absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion plays a vital role in biomedical and pharmaceutical applications, including drug delivery and its targeting. The most important route for the delivery of QDs is parenteral, but intradermal and inhalation modes are also possible.
•
Absorption of QDs—At cellular level, absorption of QDs takes place via receptor-mediated endocytosis [38]. Studies have found the accumulation of QDs in selected target tissues on i.v. administration [39].
•
Distribution of QDs—Blood environment is considered to be the first element that parenterally administered QDs will come into contact with. Very little or no information regarding blood–QD interactions is available.
•
Metabolism of QDs—The core of QDs are not occupied exclusively in enzymatic metabolism, however the shell and coating appear to decompose in photolytic and oxidative conditions which releases toxic cadmium cores [40]. Also, very little information is available on their degradation products or their biological effects.
•
Excretion of QDs—They are usually excreted in plasma proteins, however the interaction between QDs and plasma protein is still not known [41].

Due to lack of data available on removal of QDs by parenteral route, excretion poses yet another pharmacological hurdle for its research. Excretion of QDs will be dependent upon its size, physicochemistry, and nature of the coatings. Numerous studies imply that QDs can be easily removed by kidneys if the size is less than approx. 5nm [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Quantum Dots in Biomedical Field
ce:section.ce:para: QDs differentiate themselves from other nanomaterials in their pervasive prospects in numerous domains of biomedicine. Their nanosize, fluorescent crystal semiconductor nature with exceptional photophysical and photochemical properties, superior brightness, unchanging photostability, and exceptional capabilities of multiplexing in conjugation with their intrinsic symmetric and narrow emission bands have rendered them prominent alternatives for convention organic dyes in prevailing diagnostic assays. The abovementioned properties, combined with the development of methods to solubilize QDs in solution and to tag them with biological molecules, have led to an remarkable growth in their applications in biomedical fields [42]. They have been extensively studied since 1998 for various applications such as imaging of live and fixed cells to fluoroimmunoassays [43].

Many studies have shown the use of QDs as a probe in receptor-mediated endocytosis, DNA hybridization, to check parasite metabolism, and visualization of tissue and cellular structures in real time. They have been used as flurophores for in vivo applications, but their safety is still a topic of discussion. During initial stage of embryonic development of Xenopus embrios, QDs were found to be stable, nontoxic, and resistant to photobleaching on injecting [30]. This resulted in scrutinizing the development and processes of cellular differentiation that arise during embryogenesis. QDs as fluorophores are an important aid for real-time tumor diagnosis. Studies conducted with QDs have shown promising results in subtle detection of cancerous tumors and in vivo systems [44,45]. QDs free of heavy metals such as zinc-based QDs, can prove to be more promising in the near future. Injecting cells labeled with QDs in small animals and using fluorescence to follow a particular pathway can help to understand the process and discover new kinds of treatments. QDs offer various applications in the biomedical field such as in disease diagnosis, in immune-based studies, e.g., immunohistochemistry and immunoassays, in nucleic acid detection and genetic polymorphism, and in proteomic- and cellular-based applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Quantum Dots in Disease Diagnosis
ce:section.ce:para: QDs when tagged with antibodies that are specific against specific tumor markers help in cancer detection using human serum, tissue and other body fluids due to their size similarity with individual biomolecules. It is always a challenge to eradicate cancer after metastasis of cancer cells. There are several methods employed for cancer diagnosis such as chemotherapy, immunotherapy, surgery, medical imaging, enzyme linked immunosorbent assay and tissue biopsy etc. but these methods can be used only for the detection of early stage cancer with less sensitivity [46]. QDs are more preferred as compared to traditional fluorophores, e.g., organic dyes and fluorescent proteins because of the narrow absorption range, broad emission spectra and short fluorescent lifetimes. Absorption and emission wavelengths of QDs are tunable by particle size. They can be used as in vivo and in vitro fluorophores in various biological and medical applications such as live tissues imaging, detection and therapy of various diseases, including cancer.

Tumor markers such as endogenous proteins, nucleic acids, etc. including cytogenetic parameters present in body fluids, its levels are representative of the tumor state, its progress with time and response to various therapies [47,48]. The most challenging task in prognosis of cancer is development of link connecting the cancer biomarkers with clinically developed therapies for early stage cancer detection using noninvasive route. Impactive tumor markers are of choice as they can enable cancer diagnosis at primary stages. A list of tumor biomarkers are presented in Table 34.2 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of QDs in Early Diagnosis of Cancer
ce:section.ce:para: Treatment of cancer becomes really difficult after metastasis of cancer cells into a tumor of particular size. Many materials have been tried for preparing QDs, but cadmium sulfide, and cadmium selenide are considered to be the best. Major drawback associated with these materials is the leaching of cadmium atoms in the biological system which makes them highly toxic. Hence, there is a need to employ numerous encapsulation techniques to improve biocompatibility followed by bioapplicability of these nanoparticles [51]. Semiconductor QDs due to their excellent optical properties have proven their potential in the field of biomedicine (Fig. 34.1 ).

Light wavelengths ranging from UV to the infrared region can be attained with change in the size and conformation of QDs. This makes them highly suited for the concurrent examination of various molecules and events [53]. QDs are widely put to use in anticancer therapies. This is possible because it is easy to manipulate surface chemistry of QDs either by conjugating them with antibodies, ligands or other biomolecules, and by altering their optical and magnetic properties [54]. High surface area to volume ratio makes QDs more complex multifunctional nanostructures. Conjugation of antibodies to QDs allows specific recognition and tracking of surface antigens [55,56]. QDs are capable of transferring energy easily hence the light energy falling on them passes along to a nearby molecule making them to utilize this energy to show fluorescence.

Self-illuminating QD conjugates luminesce with the help of bioluminescence resonance energy transfer (BRET) in lack of a foreign excitation. During this, energy from a light-emitting donor molecule is transferred to a nearby acceptor fluorescent molecule nonradioactively which results into an enhanced sensitivity in bioimaging. Wu et al. in 2003 have identified live breast cancer cell with the aid of QDs tagged to immunoglobulin G and streptavidin which was used to label the markers present on the surface of live breast cancer cells. Antibody conjugated with QDs helps in real time imaging and tracking of single receptor molecules on the living cells surface with enhanced sensitivity and better resolution [57]. Hence, in comparison with other assays that are costly, time consuming, and labor intensive, technologies making use of QDs prove to be an economic, easy, and allow rapid detection of cancer markers. The stage at which malignancy is perceived becomes very critical for the development of assay for cancer especially for breast cancer in women and prostate cancer in men which makes early screening and prognosis of cancer highly important, thus triggering need of QD based diagnostic systems. [58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Techniques Employed for Detection of Cancer
ce:section.ce:para: In biomedical field, several techniques are used for the detection of numerous types of cancers. However, most commonly used techniques are PDT and sentinel lymph-node biopsy. Earlier, conventional dyes like fluorescent dyes were used in these techniques but now they are replaced by QDs due to their advantages over conventional dyes.

The sentinel lymph node (SLN) is the hypothetical first lymph node draining a cancer. It is assumed that the SLN is the target primarily reached by metastasizing cancer cells from the tumor. SLN biopsy (SLNB) is a surgery that removes lymph node tissue to determine whether a known cancer has spread from the original cancer site or not. Now days, this is used as a standard technique in breast cancer surgery (Fig. 34.2 ).

This method is based on determining the spreadability of disease by targeting first draining lymph node at the cancer site. Presence of cancer in nearby lymph nodes and to other organs is indicated by positive SLNB while a negative SLNB shows absence of cancer. QDs that emit in the near-infrared (NIR) range (700–900nm) have maximum tissue penetration and least tissue autofluorescence interference (emission between 400 and 600nm) and are used as a replacement for blue dye and radioisotopes due to their certain limitations. Thus, QDs can be used as a tool to overcome background tissue autofluorescence which is considered to be main problem associated with imaging of live animal [59].

Photodynamic therapy is one of the primary advancement that took place in minimal invasive therapies for the treatment of cancer and currently it is widely employed instead of surgery for the treatment of various types of superficial malignancies such as skin carcinoma (basal cell), esophageal, oral and lung cancers etc. [60,61]. In this therapy, diseased tissue is destroyed by the process of oxidation and degradation of cellular components is carried out using cytotoxic singlet oxygen created from a nontoxic photosensitizer which is activated through a specific wavelength light in the presence of molecular oxygen. Cellular necrosis and apoptosis of target cells is due to singlet oxygen (Fig. 34.3 ).

High photostability, high quantum efficiency, greater molar extinction coefficients and tunable emission spectras in the NIR region make QDs ultimate donors for the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process which helps further to study the exact site of cancer for working out the explicit targeted action [62].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cancer Using Quantum Dots
ce:section.ce:para: QDs being photostable have become an ideal candidate for the multi colored imaging and scrutinizing various processes in the living cells. For early, accurate cancer cells detection, dendrimer/QD NCs were used [63]. This finding is of importance as large number of CdSe/ZnS QDs were assembled onto dendrimer NCs owing to many functional amine groups of NCs that amplifies QDs electrochemiluminescence signals. Cancer cells and blood vessels were also targeted using QDs by conjugating surfaces with specific peptides [64].

Encapsulation of QDs with ABC triblock copolymer demonstrated the multiplexed fluorescence imaging of human prostate cancer biomarkers in mice because of efficacious binding of the QDs-antibody to tumor specific antigens [65]. Tables 34.3 and 34.4
deals with detection of cancers using QDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Quantum Dots in Immuno Based Studies
ce:section.ce:para: Despite the various advantages of QDs, they are still not considered as a standard fluorophores for diagnostic applications even though toxicity related concerns have been fixed using appropriate surface coatings. Härmä et al. 2001 established a fluoroimmunoassay for the prostate-specific antigen (PSA) detection. This assay makes use of 107nm streptavidin coated QDs containing β-diketones entrapping N30,000 europium molecules. Biotinylated PSA showed 0.38ng/L as detection limit. Detection of PSA was attained in both solid and liquid phases and individual PSA was also visualized using fluorescence microscope [80].

In 2016, Hildebrandt et al. conducted a study in which performance of three commercially available QDs with a thick polymer polyethylene glycol (PEG) coating and surface amino functionalization was tested in homogeneous FRET immunoassays. QDs were successfully labeled using a facile and generic conjugation method with fragmented F(ab) antibodies against PSA, which were then used with Lumi4-Tb-AB conjugates for Tb-to-QD FRET assays. With increase in concentrations of PSA, increase in time-gated FRET signals were observed with all three Tb-to-QD FRET systems. PSA in 50µL serum samples showed limit of detection (LODs) in the subnanomolar range. Results also show a direct proportion between the FRET parameters and the analytical performance of the FRET-pairs. The proof of concepts for three-color Tb-to-QD FRET and two-color duplexed Tb-to-QD FRET detection for biomarker immunoassays in serum samples have resulted in the development of multiplexed QD-based FRET immunoassay kits for clinical use [81].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of QDs in Biolabeling and Bioimaging

ce:section.ce:para: QDs functionalization with biomolecules is a crucial move that allows QDs to particularly recognize and bind to the cognate biomolecular targets. Various techniques to conjugate biomolecules to the QD surface have been established. These methods can be categorized into covalent and noncovalent conjugation. Covalent conjugation makes use of different chemical groups such as amine, carboxyl, thiol, etc. [66,82,83], while noncovalent conjugation involves electrostatic interaction, metal chelation, and biotin–avidin binding. Electrostatic interaction to bind negatively charged protein molecules or nucleic acids to positively charged QDs could be used [84–86]. Protein/peptides with strong metal ion-chelating moieties could attach to the QD surface directly. This can be explained with the help of examples. A polyhistidine when fused with protein molecules, was found to serve as a linker to conjugate proteins to QDs’ surface because of high affinity of histidine residues with zinc and nickel ions [87]. Protein and DNA molecules can also be used as ligands to synthesize biofunctionalized QDs. Conjugation of Biotin-avidin is another solution to bind biomolecules onto QDs’ surface [88–90].

QDs, once functionalized with appropriate biomolecules, can serve the purpose of cell labeling and imaging thereof. Owing to the sturdy photoluminescence and extraordinary photostability properties, QDs result in concentrated and persistent signals as compared to most of the conventional organic fluorophores. This QDs-based approach also helps in the single-particle tracking into the cells and developing multicolored imaging [88–90]. QDs now find an extensive use in receptor labeling on the cell surface, intracellular tracking and sensing of the biomolecules, organelle targeting, etc. Numerous types of ligands of biomolecule origin for specific labeling have been used in order to functionalize QDs. The commonly used ligands are proteins, antibodies, peptides, aptamers, and other trivial biomolecules. These ligands generally are of use in labeling the cellular surface proteins and the overexpressed receptors. For example, PSA, Her2, epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) receptors which are overexpressed on various cancer cells could be tagged with biofunctionalized QDs and hence diagnoses for various cancers can be carried out [91]. Highly specific nucleic acids aptamers through in vitro selection are picked up from an enormous RNA/DNA library either with the aid of surface cellular proteins or the cells wholly as targets. These aptamers are stable, easy to manipulate, and easy to fabricate when compared to antibodies.

Although the cellular surface labeling with QDs is readily achieved, precise intracellular labeling with QDs in live cells is much more challenging and unresolved so far. QDs in comparison to the conventional small organic fluorophores do not penetrate the cellular membranes owing to their relatively big size. Rather, the internalization into the cells is via endocytosis followed by trapping into the late endosomes and lysosomes [66,90]. Attempts have been made for the cytosolic delivery of QDs by tagging them with cell penetrating peptides or by the use of liposome-based transfection agents. Significant aggregation and endosomal sequestration of QDs were perceived for both of the methods [92]. A number of strategies have also been developed to overcome the barrier in delivery of QDs to the cytosol.

Polyethylenimine-based polymers capable of endosomal disrupting were capped onto the surface of QDs with an aim to overcome endosome and lysosome. The aforementioned polymers work by absorbing protons in the acidic partition thereby creating osmotic pressure. Thus the membrane is disrupted and QDs enter the cytosol [93,94]. However, pronounced toxicity is one of the limitations of using these polymers. Another technique for cytosolic delivery of QDs is based on the use of nanospheres fabricated out of biodegradable polymers [95]. Encapsulation of QDs in nanospheres and delivering the same into cell cytosol has been accomplished but microinjection on the other hand remains to be one of the most effective ways to deliver QDs into cell cytosol despite being tedious.

Wu et al. compared the fluorescence intensity changes of QDs compared to the organic dye Alexa Fluor 488 concurrently in the 3T3 cells with the same excitation wavelength for about 3minutes. Due to more photostability as compared to other organic dyes, Alexa Fluor 488 was selected. Futher, the photostability of Alexa 488–streptavidin and QD 608–streptavidin with specimens mounted in glycerol/Vectashield (antifade mounting medium) was compared. A 50% decrease in intensity of Alexa 488 at 10seconds and 10% decrease in intensity at 60seconds were observed without antifade medium. However, ~80% of the initial intensity at 60seconds and 55% at the end of 3minutes was retained with antifade reagents. Glycerol mounted specimens showed 104% intensity as compared to initial intensity for QD 608–streptavidin and 97% for antifade medium mounted specimens at the end of 3minutes [57] (Fig. 34.4 ).

The results have shown that despite antibleaching protection, Alexa 488–streptavidin proved to be much less photostable than QD-streptavidin. Even if protection is provided by the antifade mounting medium to the conventional organic dyes against photobleaching for live cell imaging, the same cannot be used. Hence, photostable QDs will be a prerequisite for live cell imaging and quantitative fluorescent molecular detection.

Owing to the low quantum yield and ruinous photostability, the use of organic fluorophores such as indocyanine green in NIR fluorescence imaging is restricted. However when it comes to the high luminescent NIR QDs the application is tremendously increased in areas such as in vivo imaging of blood vessel imaging, mapping of the lymph node, tumor targeting, etc. [89]. Owing to the better tissue penetrability and detection up to ~1cm in deep tissue NIR light has been favored compared to visible light [95]. QDs are now becoming trend setters in the field of in vivo cancer detection using xeno-grafted tumor models of small animals [65]. Another noticable in vivo application of QDs is their aid in imaging of the lymph nodes wherein NIR QDs, owing to their tissue permeability properties, allow real-time imaging of SLNs that are located 1cm deep. This provides surgeons with undeviating graphical supervision throughout the procedure of mapping of the SLN, thus minimizing unnecessary incisions and other associated inaccuracies.

Recently, it was realized that QDs could serve for in vivo imaging in the second biological window ranging 1000–1400nm [96]. In comparison to that of the first biological window which ranges from 700 to 900nm, the fluorescence imaging was more appealing in the second biological window. This was mainly due to the deeper tissue penetration, reduction in the photon scattering, and minimal autofluorescence. With QDs as imaging contrasting agents, two- and three-photon excitation microscopy have been developed. For the two-photon excitation microscopy, two low energy photons of the infrared light range are absorbed resulting in the excitation of QDs and generation of a photon with high energy. The aforementioned two-photon excitation microscopy which uses QDs has been applicable in various fields such as high resolution in vivo imaging studies of blood vessel. Three-photon excitation microscopy using QDs has also found its way in the field of in vivo tumor imaging [97]. The lately developed self-illuminating QDs are now contemplated to be better probes for in vivo animal imaging. BRET and chemiluminescence resonance energy transfer-based self-luminating QDs have recently been applied positively to in vivo animal imaging [98] as these QD probes do not depend upon light excitations, thus the imaging sensitivity is tremendously improved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Quantum Dots in the Field Biosensors and Biodetection
ce:section.ce:para: Numerous exceptional properties establish the potentials of QDs to be versatile biosensors in the field of detection of numerous molecules, such as metal ions, large proteins, nucleic acids, and other small molecules. QDs could also be used to monitor pH, temperature, oxygen, etc.

QDs have been employed as fluoro tags towards the detection of proteins. The prime principle involved in the protein detection with the aid of QDs is similar to that of customary immunoassay techniques [99]. A number of novel detection strategies for proteins have been established recently. These expansions include DNA-related protein detection [100], dual-aptamer-related protein detection, protein discriminating QD-based multidimensional sensing platform, ratiometric QD protein sensor, electrochemiluminescent immunosensor, etc.

Protease, an important biomarker used for many diseases is actively upregulated in several diseases and could be detected with the aid of QD-based biosensors. In this arena FRET-based QD biosensors are admired for their protease activities [101]. The FRET acceptor is linked to QDs via a peptide linker. On treatment with a certain protease, the peptide linker is smitten and the FRET signal is congested. A number of FRET acceptors are employed for the construction of the pairing of FRET with QDs. These include organic fluorophores, fluorescent proteins, gold nanoparticles, quencher dyes, etc. Multicolored QD-based biosensors have also been employed for multiplexed detection of different protease. For instance, FRET and BRET were also used in the detection of activity of protease, wherein bioluminescent proteins acted as BRET donors while QDs were employed as BRET acceptors [102].

Resilient photoluminescence along with multiple color properties make QDs an important tool for multiplexed detection of various DNA targets [103]. FRET strategy has been one of the well-known methods for DNA detection. In order to improve the detection sensitivity, enzymes are used to amplify the target. Other than FRET, detection could be carried out with the aid of a QD-labeled DNA probe and magnetic nanoparticle-tagged DNA probe with the DNA focus across hybridization. A novel labelless strategy has also been exploited for highly subtle DNA detection based on QD doping.

Biosensors based on QD have been widely put to use for the purpose of detection of many small biomolecules including nucleotides, amino acids, NADH, glucose, dopamine, ascorbic acid, etc. Electrochemistry and FRET-based fluorescence microscopy are the most ordinarily used approaches for the detection of small biomolecules with the aid of QD biosensors.

QDs play a vital role and offer solutions for applications which necessitate more than four retrieval fluorescence signals. The same on the other hand is a noteworthy challenge for conventional dyes. However, in the case of multiplex detection, generally the signals from conventional dyes intersect owing to the broad emission spectra. This is not the case with QDs which are generally known to produce intense emission bands.

The QDs are coupled with a DNA template and further assorted with C, G, and A deoxyribonucleotide triphosphate (dNTPs) in supplementation to deoxyuridine triphosphate (dUTPs) tagged with Texas Red acceptor. With the proceeding telomerization process, the Texas Red acceptor is conveyed to a particular distance from the QDs in order to undergo FRET. The same can be monitored and is a reflection of the telomerase activity [103].

QDs have also been explored for profiling of microRNA. According to one of the studies, miRNAs tagged with biotin and mongrelized with complementary oligo-DNA probes immobilized on glass slides were identified by computing the fluorescence of streptavidin-labeled QDs bound to miRNAs [104].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of Quantum Dots in Pharmaceutical Field

ce:section.ce:para: Theronostics represent those classes of formulations which serve both diagnostic and therapeutic purpose. In recent years, cohesive nanotherapeutic systems along with delivery of the targeted therapeutic moiety has gained attention in the clinical oncology arena. This interest led to the birth of manipulations in emerging nanotechnology to assume multiple functionalities [105–108]. However, in this respect the use of QDs as image contrasting agents provides enhanced in vitro and in vivo assessment of tumors as compared to traditional fluorophores [109]. In order to be used as theranostics, QDs have to be functionalized by cross-linking the same either with drug–ligand conjugate or both drug and ligand. The targeting ligands can be biomolecules, such as antibodies, aptamers, or other chemical ligands which are specific to the receptors in the targeting area. A variety of methods have been tried over the last few decades to successfully employ QDs in the theronostic field by functionalizing them with ligands [110–118]. However, a few commonly used methods for functionalization involve approaches such as thiol exchange with molecules containing sulfhydryl group or proteins/peptides with cysteine residues; mercapto acid coating of QDs, where the carboxylic acid group of mercapto acid forms stable amide bonds with amines of various targeting ligands; streptavidin-modified QDs as they can be linked to biotin-tagged biomolecules; saline chemistry applied to silica coated QDs; coupling agents such as carbodiimide and N-hydroxysuccinimide etc. [110–118]. Guo et al. in their research work demonstrated the use of CuInS/ZnS QDs as theranostic nanomedicine possessing both imaging and therapeutic capabilities within a nanomoiety. The above QDs had a potential of being used in multispectral optical tomography system for scanning and were also explored for their photoinduced tumor ablation when triggered under a single 660-nm laser irradiation resulting in high therapy efficacy against tumor [119]. Similarly Guo et al. fabricated Cu2(OH)PO4 QDs having photoacoustic imaging modality along with PDT/photothermal therapy upon irradiation at NIR radiation [120].

ce:section.ce:section-title: QDs as Drug Delivery and Molecule Tracking Aid
ce:section.ce:para: With the advances in the surface functionalizing techniques in the recent years, complex nanomedicines with QDs as drug carriers have been on the rise. QDs coated with hydrophilic stabilizers, such as polyethylene glycol, mercaptoacetic acid, mercaptoethylamine, etc., readily form conjugates with the active drugs owing to the formation of covalent bonds, electrostatic interaction, amide bonds, etc., due to chemical interactions between the stabilizers and drug moieties. Moreover, the use of these functionalized QDs have negligible side effects owing to their targeting ability, distinguishing between the healthy and affected cells owing to affinity-driven self-assembly between polypeptides and semiconductor cores, improved retention, etc. [121]. By tracking the signals of the fluorescence of QDs, the targeting, delivery, and release rates of the actives can be monitored and thereby the diagnostics and the mechanistic pathways of delivery of drugs can be understood in a comprehensive manner. As agreeable tracking probes, QDs also serve as important media for tracking the interactions and the associated pharmacokinetics at the cellular level. Weng et al. fabricated targeted liposomes by conjugating hydrophilic QDs and HER-2 ligands to the liposomal surface using polyethylene glycol. However, in the in vitro cell-based studies using fluorescence cytometry detection it was seen that the liposomes had enormous selectivity towards the tumor cells with 900–1800-fold increase in the cellular uptake by the cancerous cells [121,122]. Similarly, Yamamoto et al. studied the in vivo distribution behavior of captopril–QD complex conjugated via ligand exchange method in the stroke-prone hypertensive rats. It was found that the conjugate was capable of reducing the blood pressure while the fluorescence from the QDs revealed accumulation of the same in the liver, spleen, and lung [123]. Bhatia et al. fabricated PEGlyated QDs to which siRNA and tumor-homing peptides (F3) were conjugated. It was observed that delivery of the foresaid F3/siRNA-QDs to (enhanced green fluorescent protein) EGFP-transfected HeLa cells led to substantial knockdown of EGFP signal [124].

With noteworthy advances in the arena of nanotechnology in the recent years, nanoparticles with exceptional chemical and physical properties have gained collective importance in pharmaceutical applications. Due to their size scale, unusual physicochemical properties, and high bioactivity, nanostructures of inorganic moieties have been demonstrated to be potential candidates over the traditional irritant and toxic organic antimicrobial agents [125]. Numerous inorganic nanoparticles having antimicrobial activity have been tested recently. These include a variety of metals and their oxide nanoparticles, such as silver [126], titanium dioxide, zinc oxide, etc. [125]. Although these work on the principle of generation of reactive oxygen species upon excitation using high wavelength lights, QDs have an added advantage of having size-dependent optical properties [125,127]. Upon irradiation, QDs are known to produce free radicals, the quality of which are determined by the core material of the semiconductor. These yielded free radicals are reported to be toxic to the microbes, also, the free heavy-metal ions which are liberated in the process of irradiation are toxic to the bacteria. Li et al. demonstrated CdTe QDs possessed outstanding antimicrobial activity. It was also shown in their research that the QDs are capable of binding to the bacteria’s surface and disturb the cellular antioxidative systems, instigate downregulation of antioxidative genes, and reduce antioxidative enzyme activities [125]. In the recent years there have been numerous reports about antimicrobial activities associated with QDs [125,128,129].

A major issue that is becoming a hurdle in large-scale use of QDs is their toxicity. Many QDs are found to be cytotoxic to some extent. The cytotoxicity has been perceived in numerous in vitro studies, eventually concerning the cell growth and viability. Cytotoxicity mainly depends on size, type of capping material used, dose, surface chemistry, coating bioactivity, and QD exposure route. Many mechanisms including free Cd (QD core degradation) desorption, free radical creation, and interface of the QDs with intracellular components have been evident for this cytotoxicity. Residual organic molecules have also been found to induce a toxic impact in target cells or tissues [130]. In a study, Wistar rats were nasally exposed to Cd in a concentration of 0.52mg/m3 for 5 days at an interval of 6h/day and showed that Cd-based QDs were able to cause local neutrophil inflammation in lungs with no CNS toxicity [40]. Hepatocyte culture model demonstrated decay and desorption of Cd ions upon exposure of core CdSe to an oxidative atmosphere. However, inclusion of a silica (SiO2) and ZnS shell may only curb the oxidation, but may fail to eradicate it, particularly under the UV atmosphere. Inclusion of ligand shells has been evident in Cd desorption. The origination of free radicals, particularly reactive oxygen species, has also been one of the factors of imparting toxicity. A large accumulation of QDs was seen in the spleen due to the protective impact of ZnS shell causing the release of Cd ions from the inner side. The surface oxidation of QDs can lead to the formation of reduced Cd that can be released from QDs, causing cell death [131]. Reduced toxicity was seen with PEGylated QDs, but their modification in PEG-amine renders them cytotoxic once again. The diminution of cytotoxicity in the case of QD-PEG as compared to those of unaltered QDs has been found in association to the decline in the uptake of these improvised QDs, and not merely owing to the fundamentally reduced toxicity. The techniques of how QDs are managed by cells postuptake are capricious and various intracellular fates are likely to be responsible for different toxicity. With the inadequate data available, it is very difficult to assess the true magnitude of QD cytotoxicity, also, the contributing factors and the level of their effects are difficult to comment upon.

Commercialization of QD-based products and developing suitable regulations is a challenging task allied to the medical purposes of QD-based products. Even though a lot of research is going on and QDs for diagnostic applications are commercially available, applications involving QDs for drug delivery, therapy and in vivo imaging have a long way to go before they can actually hit the market.

Drug development is in general an extremely rigorous, costly, and time-consuming process with additional years for filing for patents. By taking into consideration the unsettled toxicity issues of QDs, it is possible that a couple of decades may pass before we essentially see a QDs-centered drug delivery approach or in vivo imaging system in the market. Irrespective of so many hurdles, the market comprising the nanobiotechnology is swiftly intensifying and several institutes, namely National Institute Health, National Nanotechnology Initiative, etc., are devoted to nanomedicine and striving towards resolving QD toxicity in medical applications [132,133].

ce:list-item.ce:para: EviDots are core–shell QDs with distinctive shells responsible for the stability of the material, improvement in the quantum yield, and reduction in the photodegradation of the QDs. They are available in a variety of wavelengths which range from 490 to 2100nm.

The various properties of EviDot core–shell QDs are as follows:
a.
Stability—The NC materials are exceptionally stable given that they are constituted of inert inorganic compounds which are further steadied with the engineered shells that are resistant to photochemical impairment.
b.
Emission Spectrum—The emission spectrum of the NCs is extremely tapered as compared to that of the organic dyes. This is due to an extremely monodisperse size dissemination which is less than 5% variation in diameter.
c.
Excitation—EviDot NCs retain extremely broad excitation properties and the same can be excited with simple triggering sources This eases the process of concurrent detection, imaging, and quantification.
d.
Intensity—EviDot NCs are seen to fluorescence intensely and therefore demonstrate high quantum yields. The brightness of these NCs is generally comparable or superior as compared to the traditional organic fluorodyes.
e.
Lifetime of the fluorescence—EviDot’s CdSe/ZnS NCs generally have a fluorescence lifetime of 15–20ns which is larger in comparison to those of the conventional organic dyes. This renders the QD exclusively distinct from other sources of intrinsic fluorescence when it comes to improvement of signal to noise ratio by an order of magnitude, which can be exploited in instruments that have fluorescence lifetime competences.
f.
Surface Chemistry—These QDs possess extremely flexible surface chemistry which can be reformed for innumerable functions along with solubility in organic and aqueous solutions.
g.
Engineering NanoMaterials—EviDots can be of help in the engineering research to meet progressive needs of the era for new biotechnological fluorescent/photonic materials, photovoltaics, computing and optical devices, lasers, light-emitting diodes, etc.
They are QD composites which utilize the properties of EviDot QDs and are common isolating polymer matrix materials.
These are water-soluble QDs which are conjugation-ready for a bioactive surface. QDs functionalized with carboxyl or amine groups are obtainable in wavelengths ranging from 490 to 680nm.

ce:section.ce:para: These are water-soluble, supreme quality, expedient QDs conjugated to antibodies and proteins. Varieties such as Goat anti-Mouse, Goat anti-Rat, Goat anti-Rabbit, Biotin, and Streptavidin conjugated QDs are available in wavelengths ranging from 520 to 680nm.

In the coming future, QDs/drug formulations will gain much more interest in the healthcare field. QDs shall serve as an aid in identifying numerous categories of cancer, help in identifying the molecular mechanisms associated with various diseases, and the mechanisms of action of novel drugs. QD/drug formulation can be designed systematically for personalized drug treatment, especially in the case of diagnosis as magnetic resonance imaging and positron emission tomography contrast agents; the same can be unified into the QD/drug formulations thus allowing one to use multiple imaging modalities to verify the biodistribution and efficacy of the drug in vivo. QDs can also serve as a useful aid in the intracellular and extracellular studies and for the development of novel biochemical assays. More research is undertaken nowadays towards fabrication of supplementary luminescent, hydrophilic QDs in order to achieve better QD efficiency and better fluorescence thereafter. Research is also being undertaken towards creating a more discerning and precise approach for labeling at the biomolecular and cellular level. More focus on producing QDs with higher biosafety is also taking place.

The years to come will see the potential applications of QDs in diverse fields like molecular probes for various biological markers, such as cell surface markers/antigens, free antigens, viruses, bacteria, tissues, etc. Due to these unique, extraordinary properties, the multifunctional QDs will be widely studied in targeted drug delivery and imaging.

In the recent years, QDs have emerged as admirable candidates in the field of bioimaging, drug/gene delivery, diagnostics, and therapy owing to their low cost, low toxicity, excellent compatibility, and nanoscale profiles. However, the most promising applications of QDs are in the fields of cancer diagnosis and treatment. QDs in the recent years have also gained interest as a research trend and scientists all over the world now conclude that QDs serve as an excellent tool for diagnostic and biosensor development. Despite such promising potentials in the diagnostic and imaging field, QDs still carry certain drawbacks owing to their toxicity concerns and regulatory and commercialization disputes. However, the contemporary progression in the surface chemistry of the QDs are now rendering QDs less cytotoxic and expanding their application potentials in the area of research of cellular processes, receptor trafficking, intracellular delivery, etc. It is believed that the future research shall take care of the toxicity concerns and QDs shall emerge as a potential tool in various medical and life science applications.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Polymer therapeutics-prospects for 21st century: the end of the beginning
Quantum dots as platform for nanoparticle drug delivery vehicle design
(CdSe) ZnS core–shell quantum dots: synthesis and characterization of a size series of highly luminescent nanocrystallites

Nontoxic carbon dots potently inhibit human insulin fibrillation
One-step synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots for imaging bacterial and fungal cells
Carbon-based dots co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation-independent emission

Synthesis of carbon dots by microwave pyrolysis of polyol in the presence of inorganic ions
Chemiluminescence of carbon dots induced by diperiodato-nicklate(IV) in alkaline solution and its application to a quenchometric flow-injection assays of paracetamol, l-cysteine and glutathione
Nitrogen and sulfur CO-doped carbon dots for highly selective and sensitive detection of Hg(II) Ions

Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles derived from candle soot
Simple aqueous solution route to luminescent carbogenic dots from carbohydrates
Scale-up synthesis of fragrant nitrogen-doped carbon dots from bee pollens for bioimaging and catalysis
Preparation of carbon quantum dots with tunable photoluminescence by rapid laser passivation in ordinary organic solvents
Facile plasma-induced fabrication of fluorescent carbon dots toward high-performance white LEDs
New strategy for synthesis and functionalization of carbon nanoparticles
From diagnostics to therapy: prospects of quantum dots
Quantum dot: magic nanoparticle for imaging, detection and targeting
In vivo imaging of quantum dots encapsulated in phospholipid micelles
Probing cellular events, one quantum dot at a time
Quantum dots and their multimodal applications: a review
Optically programmable electron spin memory using semiconductor quantum dots
Excitonic artificial atoms: engineering optical properties of quantum dots
Gold nanoparticles based chemiluminescent resonance energy transfer for immunoassay of alpha fetoprotein cancer marker
Evaluation of the fundamental properties of quantum dot infrared detectors
Quantum dots in a magnetic field: Role of electron-electron interactions
Determining the size and shape dependence of gold nanoparticle uptake into mammalian cells
Targeting quantum dots to surface proteins in living cells with biotin ligase
Cadmium-containing nanoparticles: perspectives on pharmacology and toxicology of quantum dots

Review: therapeutic application of quantum dots (QD)
Semiconductor nanocrystals as fluorescent biological labels
Ultrasmall near-infrared non-cadmium quantum dots for in vivo tumor imaging
Peptide-labeled near infrared quantum dots for imaging tumor vasculature in living subjects
Review of quantum dot technologies for cancer detection and treatment

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: National Comprehensive Cancer Network, NCCN Guidelines for Patients: Biomarkers and Targeted Therapy. ⟨http://www.nccn.com/index.php?option=com_content&view=article&id=927:biomarkers-and-targeted-therapy&catid=56⟩.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Role of tumour markers in monitoring epithelial ovarian cancer
Synthesis and application of luminescent single CdS quantum dot encapsulated silica nanoparticles directed for precision optical bioimaging
Semiconductor quantum dots in chemical sensors and biosensors
Luminescent quantum dots for multiplexed biological detection and imaging
Multispectral imaging of clinically relevant cellular targets in tonsil and lymphoid tissue using semiconductor quantum dot
Quantum-dot nanocrystals for ultrasensitive biological labeling and multicolor optical encoding
Quantum dots for in vivo small-animal imaging
Immunofluorescent labeling of cancer marker Her2 and other cellular targets with semiconductor quantum dots
Commercialized multigene predictors of clinical outcome for breast cancer
Near-infrared fluorescent type II quantum dots for sentinel lymph node mapping
Semiconductor quantum dots as fluorescent probes for in vitro and in vivo bio-molecular and cellular imaging
Photodynamic therapy: a clinical reality in the treatment of cancer
Quantum dots and their potential biomedical applications in photosensitization for photodynamic therapy
Versatile electrochemiluminescence assays for cancer cells based on dendrimer/CdSe–ZnS–quantum dot nanoclusters
In vivo cancer targeting and imaging with semiconductor quantum dots
Quantum dot bioconjugates for ultrasensitive nonisotopic detection
Conjugates of folic acids with BSA-coated quantum dots for cancer cell targeting and imaging by single-photon and two-photon excitation
Endemic Burkitt's lymphoma: a polymicrobial disease?
High-intensity fluorescence imaging and sensitive electrochemical detection of cancer cells by using an extracellular supramolecular reticular DNA-quantum dot sheath
Aptamer-based detection of epithelial tumor marker mucin 1 with quantum dot-based fluorescence readout
Combination of quantum dot fluorescence with enzyme chemiluminescence for multiplexed detection of lung cancer biomarkers
Simultaneous detection of two lung cancer biomarkers using dual-color fluorescence quantum dots
Quantum dots-based immunofluorescence technology for the quantitative determination of HER2 expression in breast cancer

Evaluating quantum dot performance in homogeneous FRET immunoassays for prostate specific antigen
Antigen/antibody immunocomplex from CdTe nanoparticle bioconjugates
